_description: The ACTUAL final submission placed on the JTC platform for France vs Spain, now SETTLED with OFFICIAL platform RBP figures (superseding the earlier estimated if_yes/if_no preview values, which were close but not exact). Distinct from 05_estimates.json, which was our pre-match recommendation and diverged from the actual submission on several questions (noted per-question below).

questions.note: Submitted the no-crowd/pure-data shrinkage estimate (0.1763~0.18) instead of the Cluster-A-suppressed, partially market-referenced 0.25 -- a larger downward deviation from crowd than recommended.
questions.verdict: BIG WIN, second-largest on the slate. The flagged divergence toward the lower pure-data number was not just correct but correct by a wider margin than the recommended 0.25 would have captured -- Yamal was completely quiet (0 goals, 0 assists, 0 shots).
Locked in at halftime (Oyarzabal pen 22' before the 24' break), held to settlement.

questions.note: Submitted the no-crowd/pure-empirical scorer-share estimate (0.31) instead of the blended 0.28.
questions.verdict: Locked in at halftime (Oyarzabal #21, confirmed via ESPN roster), held to settlement.

questions.note: MAJOR DIVERGENCE (+12pp vs recommendation). Submitted the pure Elo->Poisson->Dixon-Coles model output (0.53, favors Spain) instead of the direct liquid Smarkets market anchor (0.41, favored France advancing) -- overrode RULE1/RULE2's market-priority default.
questions.verdict: WIN. The deviation from market was right this time -- pure model beat both market and our own recommendation.

questions.note: MAJOR DIVERGENCE (+15pp vs recommendation). Submitted the personal-form k=3-shrinkage estimate (0.60) instead of the tight liquid-market anchor (0.44 mid) -- explicitly the move RULE17 was retired for (upward deviation from a liquid market on personal conviction, 0-for-5/-155.64 RBP historical pattern before this match).
questions.verdict: LOSS, the only one of the three big flagged divergences that lost. Mbappe finished scoreless (0G, 3 shots, 0 SOT). Second live confirmation of the RULE17 pattern.
WIN. Breaks at 24'/69'; Pedro Porro's goal at 58' fell inside the window.

questions.note: MAJOR DIVERGENCE (-12pp vs recommendation). Submitted the shadow-tested hierarchical GLMM's raw output (0.36) instead of the market-derived estimate (0.49) -- explicitly a live test of a model not yet cleared for submission.
questions.verdict: BIGGEST WIN on the slate. Spain finished with only 2 SOT from 10 shots. The GLMM's lower number was much closer to reality than the near-coin-flip market/crowd (~0.49-0.50). First live data point where the shadow-tested SOT/corners model clearly beat the market on exactly the family it was built and backtested for.

CORRECTED from earlier live-preview estimate, which mischaracterized this as a loss. It was a small win -- our unrounded submission was fractionally closer to the correct (NO) side than crowd's, despite both displaying as 59%. France failed to score; only Spain did.
LOSS. Locked in at halftime (France subbed first, Lacroix for injured Saliba, 30'), held. The one place our own heuristic, unaided by any market, read the wrong side.

LOSS, and the costliest kind -- our single highest-conviction number on the whole slate (72%), missing by exactly one shot on target (France finished with 3, needed 4). France had zero SOT at halftime.

questions.note: DROPPED, matching the drop recommendation (real negative settled history for this exact combined-N+ phrasing: n=17, -63.34 net RBP, 53% win rate).
questions.verdict: No RBP impact (dropped). This one resolved YES, which would have paid off had we answered near/above the crowd's 50% -- worth logging honestly, but one match resolving favorably doesn't overturn a well-evidenced 17-question base rate on its own.

questions.note: DROPPED, matching the drop recommendation (zero prior occurrences of this question type ever; Kupiec power-analysis showed no test could validate a number at n=12).
questions.final_value: Officially resolved YES -- the 83' VAR check ("Other Decision Cancelled" in ESPN commentary) was counted as a genuine on-field pitchside review
questions.verdict: No RBP impact (dropped). Resolves the measurement ambiguity flagged before the match: ESPN's text couldn't distinguish booth-only from on-field, but the platform's official ruling confirms this one WAS on-field. This is now the 2nd confirmed real occurrence for this project's evidence base on VAR-review questions (previously 1-in-12 hand-checked), still nowhere near enough for a validated model, but a real, useful data point for the ongoing record.

internal_q14_first_sub_NOT_dropped: Internal Q14 (Spain first substitution) was the third recommended drop, but was NOT dropped in the actual submission -- answered at 0.60, matching our own recommended number (no market existed to disagree with). Lost, -10.64.
summary: Final: +138.32 RBP, 10 wins / 3 losses (77%) on the 13 answered questions -- one of the best single-match results of the campaign. The 3 flagged divergences from market/recommendation split 2-1 in favor of deviating: Q7 (Spain advance, pure-model divergence) and Q10 (Spain SOT, GLMM divergence, +28.23, the single biggest win) both beat the market decisively; Q8 (Mbappe, personal-form divergence) lost (-12.48), validating the pre-match RULE17 caution. Net: genuinely mixed evidence from one match, not proof either posture (trust the model vs. trust the market) is generally right -- exactly the kind of single-data-point noise the shadow-deployment discipline exists to not over-read. The costliest loss was the highest-conviction number on the slate (Q13, France 4+ SOT at 72%, missed by one shot). Both dropped questions (offsides, VAR) resolved YES -- logged honestly for the record without treating one match as overturning either the 17-question offside base rate or the n=12 VAR power-analysis finding.
